Just after 4:00 p.m., Schindler and two other occupants in the home were downstairs and watched on a surveillance system as officers were approaching. Schindler said something indicating he wasn't going to be arrested that day and ran upstairs. Schindler already had prior convictions including felony drug possession, fleeing police and DWI. His previous drug conviction prohibits him from possessing firearms, and he was wanted that April day on several felony warrants.

Wilmer Officer Jason Hay was shot in the lower torso. He was rushed to the hospital and treated for his injury. He has since made a full recovery and returned to regular duty. Earlier this year, Officer Hay received an an honorable mention from the National Association of Police Organizations and was awarded the Law Enforcement Purple Heart from the American Police Hall of Fame Foundation.

Schindler was initially charged with three counts of attempted first-degree murder, three counts of first-degree assault, and one count of possession of ammunition. As part of a plea agreement, all but one of the charges were dismissed. Schindler was sentenced to 17 and a half years in prison, with the possibility of supervised release after two-thirds of that time is served. His anticipated release date is roughly 10 years from now.
